I've been playing Dota 2 for 12 years now, off and on throughout my life it is one of the few constants. In high school, my friends and I spent many nights playing when there was only one courier. In college I played once or twice a year on my rented laptop from school. During the pandemic It was a way for my brothers and I to connect when we couldn't actually visit each other. It holds a great place in my life.

[h3]The Good[/h3]
- Gameplay is dynamic, engaging and rewarding once you get the hang of it. There are so many moving parts that when you make your first big play, there is very little that can match the feeling of satisfaction when played just right. 
- Flourishing Pro Scene: If you want to get better there are countless new and old pro players that stream or release content on Youtube to help you improve, and often times Discord chats to find players to play with.
- Semi Consistent Updates: They just released one of the biggest updates the game has seen since Talents were released in 2016, or neutral items were released in 2019, or when they DOUBLED the size of the map in 2022. Valve isn't afraid to experiment with this game, and while you can get stuck on a single patch for a long time, you can always know that Valve Devs are working on something truly different to add.

[h3]Negatives[/h3]
- Mental Health: This is a team game. I love it for that, but playing with others can be frustrating and demoralizing. You can also find yourself "chain queuing" which after multiple losses can really effect your mood outside the game. My girlfriend and I have a rule that I cant start a game after 10pm because if i lose badly sometimes I will be too angry to fall asleep or talk before bed. You have to find these ways to look inward yourself, and without barriers it can really negatively impact you.
- The Community: I said it a bit above, but if you're new to online games there is so much racism, sexism, anger etc. I've met really great people playing dota, but the brain tends to remember the bad eggs. Remember there is a mute button.
- Communication from Valve: There can be crickets from valve sometimes. They ARE working on stuff, but if you read reddit to much you can get a bit riled for no reason.

Overall, best game I've ever played by a long margin.

Great fun game if you've got friends and play around at lower ranks trying stuff out. However, this game has one of the most toxic fanbases in existence, second only to maybe LoL. If you intend on playing solo, do your sanity a favor and just avoid this game like the plague. You will be flamed, griefed, have slurs screamed at you, etc. if these players perceive even a microsecond of sub-optimal play.

Counter-Strike 2 is a continuation of the legendary shooter series from Valve, released in 2023 and completely replacing CS:GO.

This game brings new ideas to itself every month. Some ideas are successful, some are not very. The main idea remained, and that's the main thing. Was it better before? Not for everyone, for me a little, I don't know about the rest. But before, the grass was greener, and the air was cleaner. In short, this used to be my favorite game of all time. Meet Your Match ruined it by killing off most vanilla community servers and choosing to try and turn the game about fun, player freedom, and community into yet another hardcore competitive slopfest. Valve servers are only playable through the awful matchmaking system that reinforces quick stomps instead of the multi-hour long play sessions that used to make it so relaxing and enjoyable to play after coming home from school. Valve lost the plot back in 2016 and never found it again despite the community screaming at them to just put things back the way they used to be. PUBG is not perfect. I've played from the E.A. days so I've seen how bad things have been as well as seen the positives.

Pros: Game has a grounded feel, not super-crazy movement hell for more slow paced gameplay. The hit detection has been massively improved and rarely feels off now. QoL has gotten much higher without killing how the game feels. Personally, I also like the near-instant TTK as long as you have the skills to pull it off. Guns feel mostly varied and solid to use, nothing in the game besides melee weps are nearly useless.

Cons: Some games, RNG screws you so you get garbage / ring spawns on the other side of the map and you travel 15 minutes to the other side just to be instantly killed reaching the ring after 0 action. I personally haven't noticed many cheaters but you'll usually run into one within a few hrs of playtime and others seem to complain of it a lot more. Half of the maps are reaaaally not good with far too much area to span with low vehicle numbers or no cover or just flat out play like butt.

Overall it is a game that has been fixed up from the early days and somewhat forgotten since. It's a game where I instantly understand when someone doesn't like it or feel a mutual rush when a friend hits a nasty shot. There's a lot of good and bad in PUBG but I'd say with some patience you can learn to vibe here.

I have most of the cars, the legendary gear I want, and my cyberware is maxed out to the perk build I went heavy in.  I am amazed at this game's RPG element being so customizable, but I hit the limit/end game with just doing side quests.  Everyone reading this must have already played it before I did. It's hard for me to type out a full not-missing-anything-i-want-to-say review to this game, because it holds a special place in my heart.

First off, I started to play Cyberpunk AFTER all the QoL and performance patches came out, so I never really had any issues whatsoever.

The game was there, when I was in my lowest point, it quickly became my comfort game, for anytime I felt really down. The world was just alive and it felt like every single corner had something for me to explore. You can boil it down to "it's GTA, but 1st person and in a cyberpunk setting" and you wouldn't really be wrong, but it's much more to me.

The DLC is also good, had my only real bug there where I couldn't move on with a main story mission with my first character so I had to create another one to do it (which I wanted to do anyway, so I don't care as much). Give it a try if you like open world games in a grungy setting.

Tried playing this again for the first time in over two years. Couldn't recall why I hadn't played more than the first couple of story beats. Figured it was probably due to a mix of busy with work and busy playing other games.

So I go on Nexus, setup a collection of mods, and proceed to get into the game. Or so I thought. Right after the first conversation with Jackie, I realized what the stumbling block was that prompted me to spend time on other games. When not in a vehicle, you're stuck in first person. No options for Third Person. So I grit my teeth and go search for a mod that provides Third Person view, should be something out there yea?> ... Well, there is. Kind of. There's a set of mods on Nexus that do exactly that. Both buggy as all hell. Animations don't play right, sometimes crash to desktop, often character will be bald when first loading into a scene or room, as the game isn't accustomed to having to load your hair, the list goes on. And you DO need both of those mods. One provides the ... "Framework" we'll call it, for having Third Person, the other tries to fix the various animation issues created by playing a game meant to be only in First Person - in Third Person. I don't blame those modders though for not gift wrapping me a perfect fix for this. They're creating that stuff on their own time, and presumably doing the best they can. But it's been over 5 years. CDPR could certainly have corrected this failing.

And hey, it's ok if you don't see it as a failing. You can choose to be wrong and disagree with me on this. No hurt feelings. Cept for the fact that players accepting this kind of nonsense just means more of it is likely down the line.

There in lies the problem. It's an RPG, I do not get the all encompasses desire some jackals have for First Person. It works for shooters though. I guess. For some people. But more than that, I detest game devs that eliminate choice for players. Being able to choose my own keybinds is important, but being able to toggle between First Person & Third Person should be a given. Being able to pan out away from my character, should be a given. It certainly is in many RPG games over the years. For Cyberpunk I don't know if this was a hardline choice from the folks at CDPR to force players to experience their game a certain way, or just ineptitude. 

CDPR does a lot of great stuff, and props to them for dragging this game out of the buggy mess that it was at release. But refusal to implement a Third Person option in their RPG turns what might otherwise be enjoyable hours of gaming into a sloppy mess I'd rather not waste time with.

So, great game, yadda yadda, and I do recommend it, with the caveat that you should avoid it if you don't enjoy first person perspective. For $60, gamers deserve better.

Soon as I post my review, the games trailer starts playing, and I'm struck by the ironic idiocy that they show "Your" character's face, in trailers, in game you actually design your look. Why? You won't be showing up in any mirrors. In fact if you pick up a body to move it, and walk past a reflective surface, the dead body will be floating as you don't have a proper model cause why would you need one? No need to display you in mirrors etc. BUT you took the time to give your character a face at creation. Perhaps you get to glimpse yourself in cut scenes or when you get in/out of a vehicle, who cares? That's a fraction of the time you play and honestly if I'm not pausing to look at a my character I certainly don't care what the plethora of NPCs think about it. I'd say well played CDPR, but that'd just be silly.

Apex Legends used to be an incredible game—especially around 2020 and the years that followed. The movement, the gunplay, the characters, the pacing… it all came together in a way that made it one of the most exciting battle royales out there. It was even the game that kicked off my streaming journey, so I’ll always have a soft spot for it.

But somewhere along the way, the magic faded. Instead of focusing on long‑standing bugs or quality‑of‑life fixes, the developers kept pumping out new content, reworking characters that didn’t need to be touched, and releasing new legends that felt overpowered right out of the gate. The balance issues piled up, and the game slowly shifted from fun and competitive to sweaty and exhausting.

These days, the lobbies feel dominated by players who treat every match like a tournament, and that casual, chaotic fun the game used to have just isn’t there anymore. The core of what made Apex special is buried under layers of imbalance and design choices that never quite landed.

I’ll always appreciate what the game meant to me, but it doesn’t feel the same—and at this point, it probably never will.

I love a lot of what Apex Legends has to offer. The gun play is one of the best in any game I have ever played and the charachters are very fun. However I can’t recommend the game strictly on the monetization. For example before this new season (24) there were leaked mini battle passes for all of the new legends that would add a ton of progression that was desperately needed. It would have given the players something worth playing for. But what does EA do? Make you pay 10$ for each one. This isn’t even mentioning the collection events that cost 300+ dollars to complete. They also just added a new mobile game pay system that makes you pay to unlock something cheap and then to unlock something free. All the skins are also egregiously overpriced in general. It’s all there to just make you spend as much money as possible it’s trash. They don’t really care about the players they care about the money that’s it. The balance changes are often mediocre and  don’t always fix all the problems they should. EA is lucky the foundation of this game is as good as it is otherwise people wouldn’t put up with the gross monetization. If your a new player that know you won’t buy anything then maybe give it a try. Otherwise steer clear it’s not worth your time until EA gets it together and starts to care more about the player base.(which will never happen)
